   – Triggers the tip-apex optimization script whenever Net1 rates the tip as non-optimal, performing controlled “pokes” and re-imaging until atomic resolution is restored.  

3. Executes adaptive measurement loops  
   – Automatically jumps to AI-identified, defect-free areas.  
   – Positions the tip over Net3-identified adatom sites to carry out I–V sweeps (STS) or atomic manipulation.  
   – After each spectrum, it rechecks drift velocity and tip health, rebiases or moves on if the area/tip has degraded.  

4. Enables high-throughput, statistically robust data collection  
   – Harvests hundreds of I–V curves from distinct atomic sites in an unattended run.  
   – Organizes and tags data by site type (faulted vs. unfaulted, corner vs. center adatoms) for later statistical analysis.  

5. Provides an extensible, script-based framework  
   – Users can plug in new measurement routines or analysis scripts, making the module a general-purpose self-driving layer for any room-temperature SPM experiment.  

By closing the loop between AI-driven image interpretation and on-the-fly scan control, the self-driving measurement module eliminates most manual intervention, overcomes thermal and tip instabilities, and delivers reliable, atomic-scale characterization—and big data—entirely autonomously at room temperature.

The AI-SPM overcomes the two principal sources of room-temperature instability—thermal drift and tip-apex changes—by embedding closed-loop AI modules into its scan engine.  In practice it proceeds as follows:

1. Real-time thermal-drift compensation  
   • Every 10 min (or on demand) the system grabs the latest STM topograph and uses a feature-point–matching algorithm to compute pixel shifts between consecutive frames.  
   • From those shifts it derives instantaneous drift velocities in x, y (and z if needed) and then feed-forwards corrections into the piezo drive so that the tip remains locked onto the same surface region.  
   • Iteration continues until residual drift falls below ~0.2 nm, suppressing both creep and non-linear thermal wander.

2. Automated tip-apex optimization  
   • A convolutional neural network (Net1) classifies each new image into one of 11 tip + surface condition states (K0–K10), with “good for atomic resolution” defined as K0–K3.  
   • If Net1 flags the tip as sub-optimal, the system executes a scripted “poking” routine—typically indenting the tip by ~0.9 nm at 1.5 V/200 pA, then re-imaging.  
   • If still unacceptable, the poke depth is incremented (e.g. +0.15 nm) and retried until Net1 returns a “good” class.  During every poke/re-image cycle, drift compensation remains active.

3. AI-driven site-selection and adaptive scanning  
   • Two other CNNs (Net2 and Net3) run in parallel on each topograph: Net2 locates mobile versus stationary adsorbates, Net3 finds half-unit-cell boundaries and nine key atomic sites.  
   • If Net1 reports a “bad area” (e.g. defect- or adsorbate-contaminated), the self-driving script automatically shifts the scan window to a neighboring patch and retries drift and tip checks.  
   • Only when all three networks agree that the tip is sharp, the surface patch is defect-free, and the desired atomic site is identified, does the system proceed to spectroscopic (I–V) or manipulation routines.

4. Robust training by two-phase data acquisition  
   • Phase 1: The AI-SPM itself drives the tip through hundreds of poke-and-scan cycles under varied drift speeds to collect >11 000 images spanning all tip states (K0–K10).  
   • Phase 2: It uses autonomous atom-manipulation scripts to generate a further ~7 000 images rich in adsorbate/defect patterns.  
   • Together these produce 2 000 + samples for Net1, 1 100 + for Net2 and 29 000 + for Net3, so each CNN sees a broad variety of real-world, room-temperature conditions.

5. Large-scale, statistics-driven spectroscopy  
   • For site-specific STM–STS the AI-SPM loops: locate an adatom key point, check drift and tip, acquire a 3 600-point I–V sweep in ~3 s, then recheck drift/tip.  
   • Over hundreds of spectra, it applies cosine-similarity grouping and threshold filters to reject outliers (e.g. spectra taken on a freshly changed tip) and then averages the remaining data with Savitzky–Golay and spline smoothing.  

By interleaving continuous drift‐correction, CNN‐based tip‐and‐surface quality checks, automated tip shaping, and adaptive scan relocation—all orchestrated by its self-driving scripts—the AI-SPM maintains atomic-resolution stability at room temperature and delivers reliable, site-specific measurements despite thermal and mechanical instabilities.

The thermal‐drift compensation module in our AI-SPM sits at the heart of enabling stable, site-specific measurements at room temperature.  Its key functions and implementation details are:

1. Continuous feature-point matching  
   – As the microscope scans, the system automatically identifies and tracks salient surface features (e.g. adatom clusters, step edges) in each successive topographic frame using a feature-point matching algorithm.  
   – By comparing the pixel positions of matched features from one image to the next, the module computes the instantaneous drift vector (Δx, Δy, and, if needed, Δz) at the minute scale.

2. Feed-forward drift correction  
   – Rather than waiting for feedback error to accumulate, the computed drift velocity is immediately “fed forward” to offset the piezo-scanner’s next scan center.  
   – This preemptive adjustment keeps the tip–sample registration locked on the original region despite both linear and non-linear drift behavior over hours or days.

3. Real-time, iterative updates  
   – Drift estimates and scan offsets are refreshed periodically (typically every 10 min) or after each frame acquisition, depending on the user’s script settings.  
   – The cycle of “acquire image → compute drift → apply correction” repeats until the residual drift falls below a set threshold (≈0.2 nm), ensuring sub-angstrom stability.

4. Seamless integration with self-driving routines  
   – While the drift module runs in the background, higher-level scripts (tip-conditioning, site-selection, spectroscopy loops) execute without interruption in the correct coordinate frame.  
   – This tight coupling allows the AI-SPM to “chase” the same atomic site over long experiments, automatically avoid contaminated areas or defects, and perform robust, repeatable atomic-scale imaging and spectroscopy at room temperature.

By implementing this feed-forward, feature-matching drift correction, the AI-SPM overcomes one of the principal limitations of room-temperature operation—thermal drift and piezo creep—delivering the mechanical and positional stability normally only achievable under cryogenic conditions.

The AI-SPM keeps its STM tip in an optimal, atomically sharp state by running a fully automated, closed-loop routine that combines real-time image‐based quality assessment with on-the-fly mechanical reshaping and drift compensation.  In outline:  

1.  Tip‐State Classification (Net1)  
    •  Every topographic frame is passed through a convolutional neural net (Net1) trained to assign one of 11 tip/sample‐condition classes (Ki = 0…10).  
    •  Classes Ki = 1–4 correspond to “good” atomic-resolution tip states; all others indicate that the apex has degraded (e.g. blunting, adsorbate pickup, multiple‐atom terminations).  

2.  Automatic Apex Reshaping (“Poking”)  
    •  Whenever Net1 flags a non-good Ki label, the Scan Module executes a tip-conditioning script: the tip is indented into the sample surface (typically 0.9 nm at 1.5 V/200 pA).  
    •  If the first indentation fails to restore a sharp apex, the script advances the tip in further 0.15 nm steps until Net1 again reports a good class.  

3.  Iteration Until Success  
    •  After each mechanical poke the system reacquires an STM image and re-runs Net1.  
    •  This poke–scan–classify loop repeats automatically until the tip apex falls into one of the desired Ki = 1–4 categories.  

4.  Concurrent Thermal‐Drift Compensation  
    •  A feature-matching drift‐correction algorithm runs in parallel: it tracks fiducial points between successive frames to calculate minute-scale drift velocities along x, y and z.  
    •  Those velocities are fed forward to the piezo drive so that the tip always returns to the same surface region—both during normal imaging and throughout the apex-shaping cycles.  

5.  Fully Autonomous Operation  
    •  No human intervention is required once the self-driving measurement script is launched.  
    •  By tightly interleaving CNN-based tip‐quality checks, mechanical reshaping and drift correction, AI-SPM maintains a stable, atomically sharp tip even under room‐temperature conditions.

The self-driving measurement module is the “brain” that turns the CNN’s judgments into a fully autonomous STM workflow.  Rather than running a fixed, human-prescribed scan, it continually loops through three key steps:

1.  Query AI Inference  
    •  Receives the current tip/surface quality label (Net1), adsorbate/defect map (Net2) and atomic-site coordinates (Net3).  
    •  Decides whether the present tip/surface state is “good” for site-specific work or needs intervention.

2.  Environment Rescue (if needed)  
    •  If Net1 flags a “bad tip” label, it invokes the tip-apex optimization script (controlled poking until K₀–K₃ states are restored).  
    •  If the area is flagged “bad area,” it shifts to a nearby scan window.  
    •  Every few minutes it runs the thermal-drift compensation script (feature-point matching + feed-forward) to lock onto the same atomic coordinates.

3.  Measurement Execution  
    •  Once in a “good” state and over a defect-free half-unit cell, it automatically triggers the user’s experiment script—e.g. a series of I–V sweeps at corner and center adatoms.  
    •  After each measurement it re-checks drift and tip quality before moving on to the next site.

By chaining AI inference, drift correction, tip conditioning and scripted data-collection into a single loop, the self-driving module can acquire large, statistically robust data sets (STM images, I–V series, dI/dV maps, etc.) at true atomic precision—all at room temperature and with zero manual intervention.

The AI-SPM’s local site identification on Si(111)-(7 × 7) is carried out by a cascade of three purpose-built convolutional neural networks that operate on each freshly acquired, drift-corrected STM topography.  In practice the workflow is as follows:

1.  Pre-processing  
    •  Each STM image is first corrected for thermal drift (via feature-point matching) and pre-filtered to remove low-frequency background.  

2.  Net1: Tip and Surface Quality Classification  
    •  Architecture: custom CNN (see Table 1 in the reference)  
    •  Task: multi-class classification of the image into one of Ki = 0…10 states, where Ki = 1–4 (“Good” states) indicate atomically resolved tip and clean sample, and higher Ki or Ki = 0 flag tip degradation, adsorbates, defects or steps.  
    •  Output: a vector of class weights _kᵢ_; the highest weight determines whether the current image is suitable for site-specific measurements.  
    •  Performance: ~87–90% accuracy on test sets.  

3.  Net2: Adsorbate Detection  
    •  Architecture: YOLOv8-small  
    •  Task: bounding-box detection of two adsorbate types on the half-unit cell:  
       –  A₁: mobile/noise-like species  
       –  A₂: immobile, bright adsorbates  
    •  Output: coordinates and class labels of all detected adsorbates within the scan.  
    •  Performance: ~92% mAP.  

4.  Net3: Half-Unit-Cell & Atomic Key-Point Localization  
    •  Architecture: YOLOv8-large  
    •  Task:  
       –  Classify each half-unit cell as faulted (C₁) or unfaulted (C₂) using the contrast under a negative sample bias.  
       –  Detect nine atomic key-points P₁…P₉ within each half-cell:  
         •  P₁–P₃ corner holes  
         •  P₄, P₈–P₉ corner adatoms  
         •  P₅–P₇ center adatoms  
    •  Output: bounding boxes for C₁/C₂ and (x,y) coordinates for P₁…P₉.  
    •  Performance: ~91% mAP/key-point accuracy.  

5.  Integration and Decision Making  
    •  The three nets run in parallel on each incoming image.  
    •  Net1 first gates whether the image is “good”; if not, the tip-conditioning script is invoked.  
    •  Net2 flags adsorbate-contaminated regions to be skipped for spectroscopy or manipulation.  
    •  Net3 pinpoints exactly where within a clean, atomically resolved half-unit cell the corner or center adatoms lie.  

6.  Outcome  
    •  A composite “site map” of the scanned area, with tip/sample health, adsorbate locations, half-unit‐cell type, and atomic‐site coordinates.  
    •  These annotations feed directly into the self-driving measurement module, which can then perform site-specific STS or atom manipulation at the desired P₁…P₉ positions across defect-free C₁/C₂ regions—all fully autonomously and in real time at room temperature.

In our AI-SPM system, the “probing for optimal measurement regions” is implemented as a closed-loop routine that interleaves real-time drift control, tip-apex evaluation, and autonomous scan-area selection.  In practice it runs as follows:

1. Thermal-drift compensation  
   • Every minute the system acquires a small STM topograph and uses a feature-point matcher (OpenCV) to find the lateral (x,y) and vertical (z) shifts relative to the previous frame.  
   • From those inter-image displacements it computes a drift velocity vector and applies a feed-forward correction to the piezo scan coordinates.  
   • Drift velocities are updated in ten‐minute intervals until the remaining drift is below ~0.2 nm.  

2. Tip-apex state monitoring (Net1)  
   • Each new topographic image is fed to a convolutional neural network (Net1) that classifies the tip+surface into K0…K10 states.  Labels K0–K3 are “good” (clean, defect-free area with atomically sharp tip), while K4–K10 indicate either contamination, defects, step edges or a blunt tip.  
   • If Net1 returns a “bad” label, an automated tip-shaping subroutine is invoked: the tip is indented ~0.9 nm toward the surface at the set bias/current (1.5 V, 200 pA), a new image is taken and re-classified.  If still “bad,” the indent is increased incrementally (e.g. +0.15 nm) until K0–K3 is achieved.  

3. Self-driving scan-area selection  
   • A scan-module script maintains a programmed trajectory of adjacent 11.25×11.25 nm patches (in the demonstration, 45 successive regions).  After imaging one patch, Net1’s output determines whether to accept that region or shift to the next.  
   • If Net1 reports a “good” region (K0–K3) and no adsorbates or defects are detected, the system flags it as an optimal site for atomic-resolution experiments.  Otherwise it re-centers the scan on the next patch in the sequence.  

4. Iteration until optimal region is found  
   • The loop “acquire image → drift‐correct → Net1 classify → tip-shape or shift field” runs unattended.  In the Si(111)-(7×7) demonstration it autonomously traversed 45 areas, each time correcting drift, validating tip condition, and bypassing unsuitable sites, until a defect-free, atomically resolved region was located.  

Once an optimal region is identified by this routine, higher-order site-specific operations (e.g. STS or single-atom manipulation) are triggered automatically.

The AI-SPM finds defect-free atomic images by tightly coupling on-the-fly image analysis with adaptive scanning and tip conditioning.  In practice it works like this:

1. Continuous scanning + drift compensation  
   – As soon as the tip begins a new STM image, a feed-forward thermal-drift module compares successive frames via feature-point matching to compute and cancel out drift in x, y (and z).  This keeps the same physical region in view with minimal distortion, even over minutes of acquisition.

2. Real-time classification of tip/surface quality (Net1)  
   – Every drift-corrected image is immediately fed to Net1, a multi-class convolutional neural network trained on ≈2 000 manually labeled Si(111) images.  
   – Net1 assigns eleven “Ki” weights (Ki = 0…10), four of which correspond to “good” tip + clean area states (atomic-resolution achievable, no steps/adsorbates/contamination), and the rest to various “bad” conditions (tip too blunt, adsorbates present, step edge, image blur, etc.).  
   – When one of the good classes has the highest weight, the system flags the current image as defect-free and suitable for site-specific experiments.

3. Automatic scan-area shifting  
   – If Net1 instead labels the image as “bad,” the self-driving measurement script automatically translates the scan window to a neighboring region following a programmed trajectory (see Fig. 4a), then repeats drift compensation and Net1 evaluation there.  
   – This loop continues until a good-area classification is achieved.

4. Tip-apex optimization  
   – If Net1 reports a “good” surface region but the tip itself is not in an atomic-resolution state, an automated poking routine indents the tip (starting at 0.9 nm, then in 0.15 nm steps) while monitoring the resulting images with Net1.  Poking continues until Net1 again reports a “good” tip condition.

By combining thermal-drift correction, Net1’s multi-class CNN and scripted area-shifting/tip-conditioning routines, the AI-SPM autonomously “steers” itself toward and locks onto regions that are both atomically resolved and free of defects, without any human intervention.

Performing self-driving STS at room temperature is important because it finally brings atomic-scale spectroscopic measurements out of the cryostat and into the conditions where most materials and devices actually operate.  In particular:

1.  Enables Ambient-Condition Spectroscopy  
    •  Many technologically and scientifically relevant processes—surface chemistry, diffusion, biological interactions, device operation—occur at or near room temperature.  Until now, atomic-resolution STS has been almost exclusively a cryogenic technique because thermal drift, piezo creep and frequent tip-apex changes at 300 K make site-specific spectroscopy extremely difficult.  
    •  By fully automating drift correction (feed-forward feature matching) and tip-apex optimization (CNN-guided poking routines), the AI-SPM system sustains atomic resolution long enough to perform STS on targeted adatoms.

2.  Robust, Site-Specific Data Acquisition  
    •  The integrated CNNs (Net1–Net3) autonomously identify “good” tip and surface conditions, detect and avoid defects/adsorbates, and pinpoint individual half-unit cells and adatom sites on Si(111)-(7×7).  
    •  This makes it possible to collect large numbers of I–V curves—324 individual spectra in the demonstration—at exactly the same atomic sites (faulted vs. unfaulted, corner vs. center adatoms) without human intervention.

3.  Statistical Reliability in a Noisy Environment  
    •  Room-temperature STS suffers both from instrumental drift and from thermal broadening of the local density of states.  By gathering hundreds of spectra automatically and applying cosine-similarity–based outlier rejection plus averaging, the AI-SPM yields reproducible dI/dV signatures that clearly resolve known faulted/unfaulted and corner/center adatom contrasts.

4.  High Throughput and Reproducibility  
    •  Manual STS at 300 K is painfully slow and mentally taxing because the operator must constantly re-find and re-calibrate on an atomic site.  Self-driving STS reduces that burden, enabling continuous operation over tens of minutes and the rapid building of statistically significant datasets.

5.  Pathway to Autonomous SPM “Self-Driving Labs”  
    •  Demonstrating self-driving STS at 300 K shows that fully closed-loop, AI-controlled SPM is feasible under thermally unstable conditions.  The same approach can be ported to variable-temperature STMs, non-contact AFM, and other probe techniques, opening the door to large-scale, automated materials and device characterization at realistic operating temperatures.

In short, room-temperature self-driving STS marries the site specificity and energy resolution of STM spectroscopy with the ambient operating conditions of most real-world systems—while leveraging AI for drift compensation, tip maintenance, and high-throughput data collection.  It thus represents a key step toward truly autonomous, high-precision nanoscience “self-driving laboratories.”

The AI-SPM system combines real-time hardware control, deep-learning inference, automated routines, and big-data analysis to guarantee reliable atomic-scale measurements at room temperature despite thermal and tip-related instabilities. Its key elements are:

1. Real-Time Thermal-Drift Compensation  
   • A feed-forward drift module continuously matches feature points between successive STM images via OpenCV routines.  
   • From these matches it computes x, y (and z) drift velocities on the minute scale and automatically shifts the scan origin to stabilize the tip-sample alignment to better than 0.2 nm.  
   • Drift‐update cycles run every 10 min (or after each routine) to correct non‐linear creep and long-term thermal wander.

2. Autonomous Tip-Apex Optimization  
   • Net1—a CNN classifier trained on >2 000 topographs—labels the current tip/surface state into 11 categories (K0–K10), distinguishing “good” (K0–K3) atomic‐resolution tips from contaminated or blunt ones.  
   • If Net1 reports a non-ideal tip, the system automatically indents (“pokes”) the tip toward the surface under controlled bias/current, acquires a new image, and re-evaluates.  
   • Iteration of poking and CNN assessment continues until a “good” apex is restored, with drift correction enabled during shaping.

3. Multi-Task Deep-Learning Inference  
   • Net2 (YOLOv8-s) detects and boxes adsorbates—immobile bright species vs. mobile noise-like features—so that measurements avoid contaminated areas.  
   • Net3 (YOLOv8-l) finds half-unit cells and nine keypoints (corner holes, corner adatoms, center adatoms) on the Si(111)(7×7) lattice, enabling site-specific experiments.  
   • All networks achieve ≈0.90 accuracy through a “two-phase” training campaign:  
     – Phase 1: >11 000 images spanning varied drift rates, tip states, and defect densities.  
     – Phase 2: >7 000 autonomously acquired atomic-manipulation images that enrich defect/adsorbate patterns.

4. Self-Driving Measurement Workflow  
   • A Python “Scan Module” orchestrates STM scanning, AI inference, drift updates, and tip conditioning in a loop.  
   • It only proceeds with spectroscopy or imaging when Net1 indicates K0–K3 and Net2/Net3 verify a defect-free half-cell. Otherwise it shifts the scan area or reconditions the tip.  
   • This fully automated cycle eliminates human monitoring and adapts on-the-fly to thermal drift, tip changes, or new contaminants.

5. Large-Scale Statistical Acquisition & Analysis  
   • The system collected 324 I–V spectra at four distinct adatom sites (center/corner, faulted/unfaulted), each curve sampled at 3 600 points in 2.9 s.  
   • After each sweep, drift velocities and tip conditions are rechecked; a bad readout triggers tip reconditioning or area relocation.  
   • To extract robust average spectra, a cosine‐similarity filter groups the most self‐consistent curves. Noise is reduced with Savitzky–Golay and spline smoothing before computing dI/dV.  

By tightly integrating closed-loop drift correction, CNN-driven tip and surface assessment, self-driving routines, and big-data statistics, the AI-SPM secures reproducible, high-quality atomic-scale data at room temperature.

Statistical analysis in the AI-SPM’s STS workflow is the key that turns hundreds of noisy, room-temperature I–V sweeps into reliable, site-specific spectroscopic data.  Its main roles are:

1. Pre-processing and noise suppression  
   • Raw I–V traces acquired at 3.6 kpts in 2.9 s each are first denoised with Savitzky–Golay and moving-average filters, then smoothed by a 1D spline to remove high-frequency and stochastic artefacts.  

2. Quantitative consistency screening  
   • Every I–V curve is compared pairwise using a cosine-similarity metric φ.  Values of φ range from –1 to +1, with +1 indicating identical shape.  
   • Two complementary selection schemes extract the subset of curves that share the strongest common features:  
     – Overall selection: choose those traces whose mean similarity to all others (φ_mean) exceeds a threshold T₁.  
     – Reference-based selection: pick curves whose similarity to a chosen “reference” trace exceeds T₂.  

3. Extraction of representative site-specific signals  
   • From the selected “main group,” AI-SPM computes the point-by-point average I–V curve.  This averaged trace suppresses spurious variations from tip-apex jumps (≈6.3% of sweeps) and thermal drift, while preserving genuine electronic signatures.  
   • Numerical differentiation of this mean I–V yields a robust dI/dV (STS) curve for each atomic site.  

4. Uncovering subtle LDOS differences  
   • By statistically enforcing consistency over hundreds of spectra, the system reliably resolves small bias-dependent contrasts—for example, the larger negative-bias conductance on faulted versus unfaulted half-unit-cell adatoms or the relative peak positions at ≈–0.8 V.  

In short, statistical analysis in AI-SPM:  
– mitigates room-temperature noise and tip instabilities,  
– isolates the most reproducible subset of spectra,  
– produces averaged I–V and dI/dV curves with high fidelity,  
– and enables valid, site-specific STS characterization under conditions that would defeat manual measurement.

The AI‐SPM tackles the reduction in energy‐resolution and the increased noise of room‐temperature LDOS measurements by combining closed‐loop automation, high‐throughput data collection, and statistical filtering into a single, self-driving STS protocol:

1. Autonomous, high-throughput STS  
   – The system continuously performs I–V sweeps at precisely located adatom sites (corner and center adatoms on both faulted and unfaulted half-unit cells).  
   – Over 300 curves per site are acquired in one run, each sweep taking ≈2.9 s. Between sweeps the system updates its drift estimate and checks tip health.

2. Real-time thermal drift compensation  
   – A feature‐point–matching algorithm compares successive topographic images to compute x,y,z drift velocities.  
   – A feed-forward correction is applied to the scan coordinates so that every I–V curve is acquired at the intended atomic site, minimizing distortion from thermal drift.

3. Automated tip‐apex optimization  
   – Net1 (a CNN classifier) inspects each image immediately after a sweep to judge tip quality.  
   – If the tip is judged “bad” for atomic resolution (Ki ∉ {0,1,2,3}), the system issues controlled pokes to the surface until the apex is restored to a “good” state, then re‐centers on the target atom.

4. Site-specific AI inference  
   – Net3 locates half-unit cells and key atomic sites in real time, ensuring that STS is only performed on defect- and adsorbate-free adatoms.  
   – This prevents spurious contributions to the LDOS from nearby contaminants or step edges.

5. Large-ensemble statistical processing  
   – Raw I–V curves are first denoised with Savitzky–Golay and moving‐average filters, then smoothed by a 1D spline.  
   – A cosine‐similarity metric is used to rank all curves against one another, selecting the subset that shares the strongest common trend.  
   – The selected I–V curves are averaged point‐by‐point and differentiated (dI/dV) to produce a representative LDOS spectrum.

By acquiring hundreds of STS traces under actively stabilized tip/sample conditions and then extracting the dominant spectral features through cosine‐similarity–based selection and filtering, the AI‐SPM statistically washes out Fermi‐function broadening and thermal noise. This approach restores sufficient energy resolution to resolve subtle, site-specific LDOS differences (for example, the –0.8 V surface‐state peak) even at room temperature.

The AI-SPM’s autonomous STS campaign on Si(111)-(7×7) at room temperature yielded the following key findings:

1. Fully site-specific I–V acquisition

3. Distinct conductance differences between faulted vs. unfaulted sites

   • These statistically robust, site-specific spectroscopic signatures—which historically have been easier to resolve only at cryogenic temperatures—demonstrate that AI-SPM can overcome room-temperature challenges (thermal drift, tip-apex instability, Fermi broadening) to deliver high-fidelity electronic structure data.

In sum, the AI-SPM not only automated large-scale, site-specific STS at room temperature, it also uncovered subtle, reproducible differences in LDOS between corner vs. center adatoms and faulted vs. unfaulted half-unit cells, in line with prior low-temperature and photoemission studies.

The AI-SPM experimental setup can be understood as four tightly‐coupled layers—vacuum/STM hardware, control electronics/software, AI inference, and automation scripts—together enabling self-driving, site-specific measurements at room temperature.  The key components are:

1. UHV STM Hardware  
   • Home-built, room-temperature scanning tunneling microscope (STM) operating under ultrahigh vacuum (<1×10⁻⁸ Pa).  
   • Pt/Ir tips and n-type Si(111) substrates prepared to the (7×7) reconstruction by standard flash-annealing.

2. Low-Level Control Electronics & Software  
   • FPGA-based SPM controller (NI PXIe-7857R) running real-time scan loops and analog/digital I/O.  
   • LabVIEW & LabVIEW-FPGA for synchronized tip/sample control, data acquisition, and communication with a host PC.

3. AI Inference Subsystem  
   • A server-class PC with an NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU.  
   • Python + PyTorch framework running three CNN models:  
     – Net1 (custom CNN): multiclass classification of tip-apex and surface quality (11 states, ∼0.93 accuracy).  
     – Net2 (YOLOv8-s): bounding-box detection of stationary and mobile adsorbates (∼0.92 accuracy).  
     – Net3 (YOLOv8-l): bounding-box & keypoint detection of faulted/unfaulted half-unit cells and 9 atomic sites (∼0.91 accuracy).  
   • Communication via TCP sockets between the inference PC and the FPGA controller.

4. Scan Module Automation Scripts  
   • Written in Python, integrated into the STM software for:  
     a. Thermal drift compensation  
        – Feature-point matching on successive images  
        – Feed-forward correction of x,y,z drift (updated every 10 min until <0.2 nm residual)  
     b. Tip-apex optimization  
        – Automated “poke” routine: indent tip by 0.9 nm (1.5 V, 200 pA), inspect image with Net1, and repeat in 0.15 nm steps until a “good” apex state is reported.  
     c. Self-driving measurement orchestration  
        – Real-time decision logic: choose clean, defect-free regions; perform STS (I–V) at user-defined atomic sites; monitor tip health and drift; recondition tip or relocate scan if needed.

5. Two-Phase Training Data Acquisition  
   • Phase 1 (“Tip-state diversity”): manual drift + repeated pokes to collect ∼11 600 images spanning all 11 Net1 classes.  
   • Phase 2 (“Atomic configurations”): autonomous atom manipulation via the “atomic pen” to generate ∼7 300 additional topographies with varied adsorbate/defect patterns for Net2 and Net3.  
   • Datasets were augmented 5× and split 80/20 for training/validation with AdamW optimizer.

Together, these components form an integrated AI-SPM capable of autonomously compensating for room-temperature drift and tip changes, identifying atomic‐scale targets, and executing large‐scale, site-specific spectroscopic measurements—all without human intervention.

The AI-SPM’s real-time drift‐compensation is implemented as a fully automatic “scan-module” routine that continuously locks the scan window onto the same surface patch, even in the presence of room-temperature drift.  In practice it works as follows:

1. Continuous Frame Acquisition  
   – During an experiment the system periodically (e.g. every STM frame or every ∼10 min) acquires a fresh topographic image of the sample area.

2. Feature-Point Detection & Matching  
   – OpenCV routines (e.g. ORB or other feature detectors) extract a set of reliable “landmarks” (atomic protrusions, step edges or unique defect patterns) in both the new image and a reference image.  
   – A feature-matching algorithm pairs points between the two images.

3. Pixel-Shift & Drift-Velocity Calculation  
   – From the matched pairs the mean lateral displacement Δx, Δy (and if desired Δz) is computed.  
   – Dividing these shifts by the elapsed time yields a drift‐velocity vector v = (vx, vy, vz).

4. Feed-Forward Scan‐Origin Adjustment  
   – That velocity vector is sent immediately as an offset command to the piezo scanner, shifting the scan origin so that the tip remains registered over the same atoms.  
   – This feed-forward step compensates both linear and non-linear drift over long time scales.

5. Iteration & Thresholding  
   – After each compensation the system checks the residual drift; if it exceeds a user-set threshold (≈0.2 nm), another compensation cycle is triggered.  
   – Drift‐velocity is conventionally re-estimated and applied at ∼10 min intervals to track slow, non-constant drift throughout a multi-hour experiment.

By embedding this loop into the AI-SPM’s scan module, the system maintains atomic-scale registration even at room temperature, freeing it to carry out autonomous site-specific spectroscopy and imaging without human intervention.

In the AI‐SPM system the tip‐apex optimization is carried out fully automatically by cycling between CNN‐based tip quality evaluation and controlled “pokes” on the surface until the tip is restored to an atomic‐resolution state.  The sequence is as follows:

1. Net1 evaluation  
   – After each scan, the latest topographic image is fed into Net1 (a custom CNN)  
   – Net1 assigns the tip+surface state to one of Ki = 0…10 classes  
   – Ki = 0,1,2,3 are defined as “good” tip conditions for atomic‐resolution, all other Ki indicate the apex must be refreshed  

2. Tip‐shaping (“poke”) routine  
   – If Net1 returns Ki∉{0,1,2,3}, the system initiates a mechanical indent (“poke”)  
   – The tip is driven toward the surface by 0.9 nm under the usual STM set‐point (Vs = 1.5 V, It = 200 pA)  
   – This controlled impact reshapes or regenerates the apex atom  

3. Re‐scan and re‐assessment  
   – After retracting, a new topographic image is acquired  
   – Net1 re‐evaluates the tip condition on the fresh image  

4. Iteration with adjustable indentation  
   – If the tip still fails to reach Ki∈{0,1,2,3}, the poke depth is increased by 0.15 nm for the next attempt  
   – Steps 2–3 repeat until Net1 reports a “good” apex state  

5. Concurrent thermal‐drift compensation  
   – Throughout tip shaping, the drift‐correction module continuously measures inter‐image shifts via feature‐point matching  
   – A feed-forward correction (updated every ≈10 min) keeps the tip over the same sample region, preventing drift‐induced misalignment  

By closing the loop between deep-learning–based apex assessment and controlled surface indentations, AI‐SPM maintains an atomically sharp tip at room temperature without human intervention.

Below is a summary of how the three neural‐network models in the AI-SPM system (Net1, Net2, Net3) are trained, following the two-phase data-acquisition strategy and the details given in the reference:

1. Two-Phase Data Acquisition  
   • Phase 1 – Broad Diversity:  
     – Automated routine where the tip repeatedly “pokes” the Si(111)-(7×7) surface and then scans at controlled drift velocities.  
     – Captures a wide range of tip-apex conditions (K₀…K₁₀) and surface appearances (including poor atomic resolution).  
     – Yields 11 616 raw images.  
   • Phase 2 – Atomic-Resolution Enrichment:  
     – Self-driving measurements (e.g. “atomic pen” manipulation) to generate varied adatom/defect patterns.  
     – Adds 7 269 images, heavily featuring clean unit cells, adsorbates, and defects.

2. Dataset Construction & Labeling  
   • From the pooled images:  
     – Net1 (tip + sample quality) uses 2 082 labeled samples.  
     – Net2 (adsorbate detection) uses 1 105 labeled samples.  
     – Net3 (half-unit cell + atomic keypoints) uses 29 145 labeled samples.  
   • Manual verification of labels via CVAT after an initial auto-labeling pass.  
   • Images resized to 256×256 pixels.

3. Data Splitting and Augmentation  
   • Train/Validation split: 80% / 20%.  
   • Augmentation (×5 expansion) on both sets: random affine transforms, crops, contrast jitter.

4. Model Architectures & Initialization  
   • Net1: Custom CNN for multi‐class classification of tip/surface state (K₀…K₁₀).  
   • Net2: YOLOv8-small for bounding-box detection of immobile (A₂) and mobile (A₁) adsorbates.  
   • Net3: YOLOv8-large for bounding-box detection of half-unit cells (C₁,faulted; C₂,unfaulted) and nine atomic keypoints (P₁…P₉).  
   • Net2/Net3 initialized from pre-trained YOLOv8 weights; Net1 trained from scratch.

5. Training Procedure  
   • Optimizer: AdamW.  
   • Hyperparameters:  
     – Net1: lr=5×10⁻⁴, epochs=720, batch=16  
     – Net2: lr=2×10⁻³, epochs=150, batch=16  
     – Net3: lr=1×10⁻³, epochs=300, batch=16  
   • All training run in PyTorch on an RTX4090 GPU.

6. Validation & Performance  
   • Net1 achieves ≈0.93 accuracy in classifying tip/surface states.  
   • Net2 achieves ≈0.92 mAP for adsorbate detection.  
   • Net3 achieves ≈0.91 mAP/precision for half-unit cell and atomic keypoints.  
   • These accuracy levels meet the requirements for real-time site-specific decision making in room-temperature SPM.

By combining a systematic two-phase image-collection workflow, careful manual labeling, aggressive augmentation, and tailored network architectures with optimized hyperparameters, the AI-SPM trains robust deep-learning models that can (in real time) evaluate tip health, detect adsorbates, locate unit cells, and pick out individual adatoms for autonomous site-specific measurements.

In the AI-SPM’s post-processing of large sets of room-temperature I–V curves, the cosine-similarity metric is used to isolate the most internally consistent data and suppress outliers caused by tip changes, thermal drift or electronic noise.  Its implementation proceeds in three steps:

1. Pairwise similarity calculation  
   – For every pair of I–V curves I_i and I_j (each sampled at n bias points), the system computes  
     φ(I_i,I_j) = ∑ₖ[I_i(k)·I_j(k)] / (‖I_i‖·‖I_j‖)  
     where φ ranges from –1 (anti-correlated) to +1 (identical).  

2. Group selection  
   • Overall selection mode:  
     – Compute φ_mean(I_l) = (1/M)∑_{i=1…M}φ(I_l,I_i) for each curve I_l in the full set of M curves.  
     – Retain only those I_l for which φ_mean(I_l) exceeds a user-defined threshold T₁.  
   • Reference-based mode:  
     – Choose a single “reference” I–V curve I_ref.  
     – Retain all curves I_l for which φ(I_ref,I_l) > T₂.  

   By either criterion, the AI-SPM automatically culls a subset of I–V spectra that exhibit the strongest mutual resemblance.

3. Averaging and smoothing  
   – The selected subset is averaged point-wise to produce a representative I–V profile.  
   – Savitzky–Golay and moving-average filters, followed by a 1D spline fit, are applied to suppress residual noise.  
   – Differentiation then yields the dI/dV (STS) curve.  

This procedure ensures that the final I–V and dI/dV spectra used for site-specific density–of–states analysis are statistically robust and minimally affected by the variability inherent to room-temperature STM measurements.

The AI-SPM guarantees both accuracy and reproducibility by closing the loop between real-time AI inference, active instrument control, and statistical data validation:

1. Composite CNN-Based Quality Assessment  
   • Three networks (Net1–Net3) jointly evaluate every STM frame:  
     – Net1 classifies tip‐apex and surface quality into 11 states (K0–K10) with 87–90 % accuracy.  
     – Net2 detects and distinguishes stationary and mobile adsorbates (≈92 % accuracy).  
     – Net3 localizes half‐unit cells and nine key adatom/corner-hole sites (≈91 % accuracy).  
   • These models were trained on >40 000 in situ images acquired via a two-phase routine (Phase 1: broad coverage of tip states and drift conditions; Phase 2: high-resolution scans via automated atom manipulation) and augmented to ensure robustness to tip variations and surface defects.

2. Real-Time Thermal Drift Compensation  
   • A feed-forward feature‐matching algorithm tracks and matches corners and defects between consecutive scans (every ≈10 min), computes drift velocities in x, y, z, and updates scan offsets until residual drift falls below 0.2 nm.  
   • This prevents image distortion and keeps the tip aligned to the same atomic registry over long measurements.

3. Autonomous Tip Apex Optimization  
   • Net1 continually monitors tip quality. If the apex degrades (K > 3), the system executes a controlled “poke” protocol—initial 0.9 nm indent at set bias/current, with successive 0.15 nm increments—until the network again reports a “good” state (K0–K3).  
   • Tip health is automatically re-assessed after each poke, ensuring sustained atomic resolution without human intervention.

4. Self-Driving Measurement Logic  
   • The AI inference module feeds the Scan Module:  
     – It avoids regions flagged as defective or contaminated.  
     – It targets defect-free half-unit cells and specific adatom sites for spectroscopy or manipulation.  
   • All scanning, drift correction, tip conditioning, and site selection run under a unified Python/LabVIEW framework, eliminating manual repositioning or eye-balling.

5. Large-Scale Statistical Validation for Spectroscopy  
   • For site-specific I–V/STS, the system collected 324 curves across four adatom types in one run.  
   • Each raw curve is noise-filtered (Savitzky–Golay + mean filter + spline smoothing) and then grouped by cosine‐similarity to extract the most representative trend.  
   • Averaging over tens to hundreds of spectra suppresses outliers from thermal broadening or transient tip changes, yielding statistically robust dI/dV profiles.

By combining highly accurate, in situ-trained neural networks with closed-loop drift and tip control—and by generating and statistically vetting large datasets—the AI-SPM achieves reproducible, atomic-scale measurements at room temperature.

Integrating AI into SPM fundamentally transforms it from a manually steered microscope into a self-driving, high-throughput platform.  The key benefits demonstrated in the AI-SPM work on Si(111)-(7×7) are:

1. Autonomous Tip-Health and Drift Management  
   • A CNN (Net1) continuously classifies tip-apex and surface conditions (good/contaminated) with ≈90 % accuracy.  
   • When the tip degrades, the system automatically performs controlled “pokes” (bias/current pulses plus approach) until atomic resolution is restored.  
   • A feature-point–matching feed-forward algorithm tracks thermal drift on minute-to-day timescales and adjusts the scan area in real time, keeping tip and sample atoms locked together at room temperature.

2. Real-Time, Site-Specific Decision Making  
   • A second CNN (Net2) discriminates immobile versus mobile adsorbates, while a third (Net3) locates half-unit cells (faulted vs. unfaulted) and nine key adatom/corner-hole positions.  
   • This lets the microscope autonomously avoid defect- or adsorbate-laden regions and zero in on predefined atomic sites for spectroscopy or manipulation.

3. Self-Driving Measurement Workflows  
   • Scan-module scripts read AI inferences, switch scanning areas, invoke tip conditioning or drift correction, and launch the next measurement—all without human intervention.  
   • In practice, the system can acquire dozens of consecutive atomic-resolution images or hundreds of site-specific I–V curves in a single unattended run.

4. High-Throughput, Statistically Robust Spectroscopy  
   • The AI-SPM collects large I–V datasets (e.g. 324 curves × 3 600 points) at distinct adatom sites.  
   • Automated data-selection (cosine-similarity filtering) plus noise-reduction (Savitzky–Golay smoothing) yields reliable dI/dV spectra that reveal subtle electronic differences between faulted and unfaulted adatoms.

5. Scalable, Adaptive AI Training  
   • A two-phase “self-training” routine first gathers diverse tip-state images via poke-scan loops and then expands the dataset by AI-driven atomic manipulations to introduce varied defects/adsorbates.  
   • This generated ∼40 000 annotated images used to train Net1–Net3, each reaching >90 % accuracy, and the same pipeline can be adapted to new surfaces, AFM modes or cryogenic STM.

6. Reduced Human Error and Increased Reproducibility  
   • By automating drift correction, tip repair, defect avoidance and data curation, AI-SPM removes routine manual tasks and user bias, ensuring consistent, reproducible atomic-scale experiments at room temperature.

7. New Experimental Possibilities  
   • Fully autonomous, atomic-precision measurements under ambient conditions open the door to studying thermally activated processes (diffusion, crystal growth, chemical reactions) in real time.  
   • AI-SPM lays the groundwork for true “self-driving laboratories” in nanotechnology, from materials discovery to automated atomic manipulation.

In sum, AI integration delivers autonomous control, site-specific precision, high throughput and statistical rigor, overcoming long-standing challenges (thermal drift, tip instability, manual labor) and setting a new standard for atomic-level surface analysis.
